coredata.dc:description: Quercetin, a flavonoid known for its antioxidant properties, has recently garnered attention as a potential neuroprotective agent for treatment of the injured nervous system. The repair of peripheral nerve injuries hinges on the proliferation and migration of Schwann cells, which play a crucial role in supporting axonal growth and myelination. In this study we synthesized Quercetin-derived carbon dots (QCDs) and investigated their effects on cultured Schwann cells and the NG108-15 cell line. QCDs was obtained by solvothermal synthesis and characterized via UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction analysis. The particles demonstrated significant dose-dependent free radical scavenging activity in DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging assays, supported in vitro proliferation and migration of Schwann cells, expression of neurotrophic and angiogenic growth factors, and stimulated neurite outgrowth from NG108-15 cells. Thus, QCDs could serve as a potential novel treatment strategy to promote regeneration in the injured peripheral nervous system.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of QCDs with transmission electron microscopy (TEM). (A) TEM images of QCDs and (B) particle size distribution histogram obtained from 70 individual QCDs. The samples were prepared by drop casting 0.1 mg/mL ethanol solution on a carbon support grid.

Characterization of QCDs with X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR). (A) XRD spectra and (B) FTIR spectra of quercetin and QCDs. XRD were measured from a drop-casted film made from 0.1 mg/mL ethanol solutions of both quercetin and QCDs. Sample pellets for normalized FTIR spectra were prepared by mixing the dried powder of quercetin or QCDs with FTIR standard potassium bromide powder.

Photophysical properties of QCDs. (A) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra of quercetin and QCDs in ethanol solution. (B) PL spectra of quercetin and QCDs in ethanol solution, at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. All solutions used for spectral measurement were at a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL.

Antioxidant activity of QCDs. (A) ABTS scavenging assay and (B) DPPH scavenging assay of the quercetin and QCDs at different concentrations. The data represents the mean ± SEM. P-values are indicated as follows: ns, non-significant *p ≤ 0.05, **p ≤ 0.01, ***p ≤ 0.001, ****p ≤ 0.0001.

Live/Dead assay and AlamarBlue cell proliferation assay. (A-H) Live/dead cell assay of Schwann cells treated with different concentrations (0, 0.15 mg/mL, 0.3 mg/mL, 0.75 mg/mL) of quercetin (A-D) and QCDs (E-H). Representative images of live cells (green fluorescence) and dead cells (red fluorescence) 24 h after treatment. (I) Histogram demonstrating Schwann cell proliferation at 1-, 3-, and 5 days in control cultures and following treatment with quercetin (QUER) and QCDs at different concentrations (0.3 mg/mL, 0.15 mg/mL, and 0.75 mg/mL). The data represents the mean ± SEM. P-values are indicated as follows: * p ≤ 0.05, ** p ≤ 0.01 and **** p ≤ 0.0001. Scale bar, 100 μm.

Wound healing assay and migration assay. (A-H) Wound healing assay demonstrating Schwann cells migration at 0 h (A-D) and 24 h (E-H) without treatment (Control) and following treated with QCDs at different concentrations (0.15 mg/mL, 0.3 mg/mL, 0.75 mg/mL). Dashed lines indicate the borders of the injury. (I-L) Representative images showing the results of Schwann cell migration assay through the 8.0-µm pores of the culture inserts at 24 h without treatment (Control) and following treated with QCDs at different concentrations (0.15 mg/mL, 0.3 mg/mL, 0.75 mg/mL). (M, N) Histograms with quantitative analyses of the wound healing assay and migration assay. The data represents the mean ± SEM. P-values are indicated as follows: * p ≤ 0.05, *** p ≤ 0.001 and **** p ≤ 0.0001. Scale bar, 100 μm.

Expression of neurotrophic and angiogenic factors. (A-E) qRT-PCR analysis of neurotrophic factor expression in Schwann cells treated with different concentrations of QCDs. The data represents the mean ± SEM. P-values are indicated as follows: *p ≤ 0.05, **p ≤ 0.01, ***p ≤ 0.001 and **** p ≤ 0.0001.

Co-localization of QCDs with NG108-15 cells and neurite outgrowth assay. (A-C) Visualization of the QCDs (0.75 mg/mL) in close association with NG108-15 cells after 24 h in culture The cells were imaged using brightfield microscopy (A) and fluorescent microscopy (excitation wavelength, 412 nm). (D-H) Representative images of NG108-15 cells stained with βIII-tubulin antibody and histograms showing effects of QCDs (0.3 mg/mL and 0.75 mg/mL) on the number of cells with neurite outgrowth and the length of neurites. The data represents the mean ± SEM. P-values are indicated as follows: **p ≤ 0.01 and ****p ≤ 0. 0001. Scale bar, 50 μm.
PCR primers are used to quantify mRNA expression levels.

head.ce:title: Quercetin-derived carbon dots promote proliferation and migration of Schwann cells and enhance neurite outgrowth

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quercetin, a flavonoid known for its antioxidant properties, has recently garnered attention as a potential neuroprotective agent for treatment of the injured nervous system. The repair of peripheral nerve injuries hinges on the proliferation and migration of Schwann cells, which play a crucial role in supporting axonal growth and myelination. In this study we synthesized Quercetin-derived carbon dots (QCDs) and investigated their effects on cultured Schwann cells and the NG108-15 cell line. QCDs was obtained by solvothermal synthesis and characterized via UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction analysis. The particles demonstrated significant dose-dependent free radical scavenging activity in DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging assays, supported in vitro proliferation and migration of Schwann cells, expression of neurotrophic and angiogenic growth factors, and stimulated neurite outgrowth from NG108-15 cells. Thus, QCDs could serve as a potential novel treatment strategy to promote regeneration in the injured peripheral nervous system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Peripheral nerve injury (PNI) is a prevalent condition leading to impaired electrical conduction and target organ dysfunction [1,2]. Young adults are particularly susceptible to PNI, which often results in poor prognoses and limited functional recovery [3]. Despite significant advances in microsurgical techniques for nerve reconstruction, enhancing the biological activity of Schwann cells remains an important factor for boosting nerve repair and offering new therapeutic options [4,5].

Denervated Schwann cells in the distal nerve stump produce different neurotrophic factors which support axonal regeneration [5]. Following PNI, the proliferation and migration of Schwann cells is crucial for effective repair, facilitating nerve regeneration by providing support for growing axons. Studies have shown that Schwann cells secrete a variety of neurotrophic factors (NTFs) that promote neuronal survival and axonal growth, highlighting their importance in the repair process [6]. Understanding the mechanisms underlying Schwann cell proliferation, migration, and their interactions with other cells in the nerve microenvironment is essential for developing novel strategies to enhance nerve regeneration following PNI. Investigating the impact of different factors, such as antioxidants and growth factors on Schwann cell behaviour can provide valuable insights into the complex processes involved in nerve regeneration.

Quercetin (3, 3′, 4′, 5, 7′-pentahydroxyflavone), a member of the flavonoid family, has recently attracted attention due to its antioxidant, cytoprotective, antinociceptive, antithrombotic, anti-inflammatory, and anticancer properties [7–9]. Although quercetin is offered to patients as a supplementary therapy for neurodegenerative diseases, the mechanisms of its effects on different cell types in the nervous are still not fully understood [10]. Previous studies show that quercetin has neuroprotective effects in various in vitro and in vivo models of neural degeneration including brain trauma [11], spinal cord injury and cerebral ischemia [12], and Huntington’s disease [13]. Quercetin has been extensively studied for its ability to attenuate oxidative stress in various cellular models [14–16]. Quercetin exerts its antioxidant effects through mechanisms including detoxification of reactive oxygen species (ROS), inhibition of ROS generation, and enhancement of endogenous antioxidant enzyme expression [14,16].

To deliver pharmacological agents to the injured nervous system, different nanomaterials have been developed [17]. Carbon dots (CDs) are carbonaceous fluorescent nanoparticles (< 10 nm) that have emerged in recent years as a unique, versatile class of advanced carbon nanomaterials. CDs exhibit chemical stability, low cytotoxicity, and cell permeability, and have been utilized in various biological applications [18,19]. CDs have been used as drug-delivery vehicles primarily owing to their bioavailability [20], molecular targeting capabilities, and solubility in aqueous solutions [21]. Studies have shown that CDs can scavenge ROS effectively, thereby preventing cellular damage and oxidative stress [22,23]. The antioxidative properties of CDs have been attributed to their ability to balance oxidant levels and enhance the total amount of antioxidants in cells [22,23]. Additionally, the functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as carboxyl groups, could play a role in their antioxidant activities by acting as proton donors and scavenging free radicals [24].
Quercetin's ability to reduce oxidative stress coupled with the antioxidative properties of carbon dots enhances protection against cellular damage and improves cellular health [25,26].

The present study aims to explore the biocompatibility of quercetin-derived carbon dots (QCDs) on the proliferation and migration of Schwann cells, expression of neurotrophic and angiogenic factors important for neural regeneration and neurite outgrowth from the NG108-15 cell line.

Quercetin hydrate (20 mg; ≥95.0 %, Sigma Aldrich), was weighed into a vial followed by the addition of 10 mL of ethanol (≥99.0 %, VWR International). For a simplification, we use the term of quercetin hereafter. The mixture was shaken via vortex until quercetin was thoroughly dispersed in ethanol. The resultant solution was then transferred into an autoclave reactor and solvothermally synthesized at a temperature of 180 °C for 10 h. The resultant mixture was cooled to room temperature and filtered with a 0.45 µm PTFE filter to remove the insolubles. Ethanol is removed via a rotary evaporator. The powder obtained was redispersed in ethanol and filtered with a 0.1 µm PTFE filter to obtain purified QCDs. Solid QCDs as the final product were obtained by drying the ethanol solution in a vacuum oven operating at a temperature of 30 °C. The QCDs were stored in a dark place under ambient conditions and room temperature (22 °C) for more than six months, and their optical properties remained unchanged (Fig. S1, Supplementary information).

The morphology of QCDs was determined with transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Talos F200X, Thermo Fisher Scientific) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements of films of quercetin and QCDs were determined with an X-ray diffractometer (X’Pert3powder, PANalytical). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, IFS/v66, Bruker) was employed in determining the functional groups present in quercetin and QCDs. Photophysical properties were investigated by UV–vis absorption spectrometer (Lambda 1050, Perkin Elmer) and photoluminescence (PL) spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments).

The 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) assay was conducted to assess free radical scavenging capacity and antioxidant activity of QCDs using a modification of the method described by Xiang et al. [27]. In brief, 55 µL of each quercetin and QCDs at varying concentrations, prepared by a two-fold dilution, were dispensed into 96-well plates. Subsequently, 150 µL of 0.2 mM DPPH solution was added to each well and the mixture was incubated for 30 min in the dark. Absorbance readings were then taken at 517 nm. Ascorbic acid and methanol served as positive and negative controls, respectively. All reactions were performed in triplicate. The percentage of DPPH scavenging activity was calculated using the formula:
DPPH radical scavenging (%) = [(Abscontrol − Abssample)/Abscontrol] × 100
where Abscontrol is the absorbance of the control reaction and Abssample is the absorbance of the quercetin and QCDs concentrations.

The 2,20-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) radical scavenging assay was based on the modified method described by Almeida et al. [28]. A stock solution of ABTS radical cation (ABTS•+) was prepared by incubating 7 mM ABTS solution with 2.45 mM potassium persulfate for 16 h in the dark. The ABTS•+ solution was then diluted with 95 % ethanol to achieve an absorbance of 1.0 at 734 nm. Ten microliters of different concentrations of quercetin and QCDs were added to 96-well plates, followed by the addition of 290 µL of ABTS•+ solution. The mixture was incubated in the dark, and after 7 min, the absorbance was measured at 734 nm. Ascorbic acid and methanol were employed as positive and negative controls, respectively. All samples were evaluated in triplicate. The percentage of ABTS radical scavenging activity was calculated using the formula:
ABTS radical scavenging (%) = [(Abscontrol − Abssample)/Abscontrol] × 100
where Abscontrol is the absorbance of the control reaction and Abssample is the absorbance of the quercetin and QCDs concentrations.

Primary cultures of rat Schwann cells were prepared as described in our previous studies [29,30]. The experiments were approved by the Animal Review Board at the Court of Appeal of Northern Norrland in Umeå, Sweden (DNR # A15-20).

Sciatic nerves were dissected from adult female Sprague–Dawley rats (n = 5), cut into 1–2 mm long pieces and cultured in plates containing Dulbecco′s Modified Eagle′s Medium (DMEM, Thermo Fisher Scientific) with 10 % heat-inactivated fetal calf serum (FCS, Sigma Aldrich) under standard culture conditions. After 2 weeks, the nerve fragments underwent enzymatic dissociation and were replated onto poly-D-lysine-coated (PDL, R&D Systems) tissue culture flasks in DMEM/10 % FCS supplemented with 50 ng/mL neuregulin (R&D Systems) and 10 µM forskolin (Invitrogen). Upon reaching confluence, Schwann cells were purified using a medium containing 10 µM cytosine-b-D-arabinofuranoside (Sigma Aldrich). The purity of cells was assessed with immunostaining for S-100 protein (1:1000; Dako) and approximately 95 % pure cell populations were used in the study.

To evaluate cell viability, the LIVE/DEAD™ Cell Imaging Kit (Invitrogen) was used. The medium of the Schwann cells containing different concentrations of QCDs (0, 0.15 mg/mL, 0.3 mg/mL, 0.75 mg/mL) or quercetin (0, 0.15 mg/mL, 0.3 mg/mL, 0.75 mg/mL) was aspirated and then co-incubated with the preconfigured mixture for 30 min. A control group without QCDs or quercetin was used as the baseline measurement. The cells were analyzed using an Olympus IX71 inverted microscope equipped with fluorescent imaging and a DS-Ri1 camera (Nikon). The concentrations of quercetin and QCDs were selected based on the cytotoxicity assay with mesenchymal stem cells (Fig. S2, Supplementary information).

To assess the effects of different concentrations of quercetin and QCDs (0, 0.15 mg/mL, 0.3 mg/mL, 0.75 mg/mL) on Schwann cell attachment and proliferation, the AlamarBlue™ assay (BUFO12A, Bio-Rad Laboratories AB, Solna, Sweden) was used. A control group without quercetin or QCDs served as the baseline measurement. At specified time points (1, 3, and 5 days), AlamarBlue™ solution (100 µL per 1 mL of culture media) was added and incubated for 8 h. Subsequently, a 200 µL aliquot of the solution was transferred into a 96-well plate, and the optical density was measured by recording absorbance at 570 nm with correction at 600 nm using a Synergy HT microplate reader equipped with Gen5 software v2.04 (Biotek Instruments, Santa Clara, CA, USA). Each measurement was conducted with four replicate samples.

Schwann cells were seeded onto 24-well plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well. Upon reaching 90 % confluence, a scratch injury was carefully created through the cell monolayer using a sterile 200 μL pipette tip, and detached cells were subsequently removed. The plates were washed twice with PBS and incubated in fresh low-glucose DMEM supplemented with 10 % heat-inactivated FCS at 37 °C, either with or without the specified concentrations of QCDs. The cell density in the scratch site was analyzed using an Olympus IX71 inverted microscope equipped with a DS-Ri1 camera (Nikon).

A 24-well transwell system with 8.0-µm pores (Corning, Inc.) was used for the migration and invasion assays. The influence of various concentrations of QCDs (0, 0.15 mg/mL, 0.3 mg/mL, 0.75 mg/mL) on Schwann cell migration was investigated. Schwann cells (20 × 104 cells in 250 µL of serum-free medium) were seeded into the inserts, while 500 µL of low-glucose DMEM containing 10 % heat-inactivated FCS and varying concentrations of QCDs (0, 0.15 mg/mL, 0.3 mg/mL, 0.75 mg/mL) was added to the lower chamber. The cells were allowed to migrate through the pores of the inserts for 24 h, after which they were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 15 min and stained with crystal violet for 15 min. Non-migrated cells within the inserts were removed using cotton swabs. Five randomly chosen fields were captured using an Olympus IX71 inverted microscope equipped with a DS-Ri1 camera (Nikon), and the number of migrated cells was quantified using ImageJ.

The NG108-15 neuroblastoma-glioma hybrid cell line (ATCC) was used for neurite outgrowth analyses. The NG108-15 cells on Labtek® eight-well chamber slides (Fisher Scientific) were seeded at a density of 1000 cells/cm2 and allowed to adhere to tissue culture plastic for a minimum of 24 h before modifying the culture media to various experimental conditions. These conditions included control DMEM media (without QCDs) and different concentrations of QCDs (0.3 mg/mL, 0.75 mg/mL) diluted with DMEM media. These treatments were directly applied to the NG108-15 cells for 24 h. Each treatment was conducted in triplicate. Following 24 h in culture, images were captured using Olympus IX71 inverted microscope equipped with a DS-Ri1 camera (Nikon) or fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde, and immunostained with βIII-tubulin antibody (1:200, T2200, Sigma-Aldrich) [22]. At least four areas with clearly defined neurons were captured using Nikon Eclipse E80i microscope coupled to a Nikon DS-U2 digital camera and traced with ImageJ to measure the longest neurites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tracking fluorescent signal of QCDs in cultured cells
ce:section.ce:para: To determine if QCDs fluorescent properties could be used to track carbon particles in cell culture, we used the NG108-15 cells on Labtek® eight-well chamber slides (Fisher Scientific) at a density of 10 000 cells/well in DMEM medium supplemented with 10 % FCS and 1 % streptomycin. The cells were incubated for 24 h after which the culture media was removed, 0.75 mg/mL of QCDs was added in DMEM media and cells were incubated for additional 24 h. The cells were washed with the culture medium and analyzed using Olympus IX71 inverted microscope equipped with fluorescent imaging and a DS-Ri1 camera.

Cultured cells were fixed in 4 % (w/v) paraformaldehyde for 20 min at room temperature. Subsequently, cells were blocked with a solution containing 5 % normal horse and goat sera in 0.1 % BSA, 0.1 % sodium azide, and 0.1 % Triton for 15 min. The primary antibodies, rabbit anti-S-100 protein (1:1000; Dako) or rabbit anti-β-Tubulin III (1:200, T2200, Sigma-Aldrich) were applied for 2 h at room temperature. The cells were washed with PBS and incubated with normal serum, followed by secondary antibody (Alexa Fluor 488, 1:300 dilution; Molecular Probes, Invitrogen), for 1 h in the dark. The cells were mounted using ProLong mounting media containing 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI; Invitrogen Life Technologies).

Total RNA was isolated from the cells cultured with different concentrations of QCDs (Control without particles, 0.3 mg/mL, 0.75 mg/mL) after 3 days using an RNeasy™ kit (Qiagen, Hilden). Total RNA was extracted using RNeasy mini kits according to the manufacturer's instructions (Qiagen). iScript cDNA synthesis kit (Bio-Rad) was used to convert 10 ng/ul reaction mix into complementary DNA (cDNA). qRT-PCR was performed with Myra Liquid Handling System (Bio Molecular Systems) using SsoFast™EvaGreen® supermix (Bio-Rad) with 1 ng of cDNA, Mic qPCR cycler (Bio Molecular Systems) and Mic PCR software (Bio Molecular Systems). All samples were analysed in quadruplicates. The housekeeping gene used to normalize the expression levels was GAPDH gene. Primers were manufactured by Sigma/Merck, sequences and variable annealing/extension temperature are displayed in Table 1. ΔΔC(t) principle was applied to calculate the relative expression data.

Statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism version 10 for Windows, (GraphPad Software, La Jolla CA, USA, www.graphpad.com). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), followed by Tukey’s multiple comparisons test, was performed to determine all statistically significant differences. Data on all graphs are presented as the mean and the standard error of the mean (SEM). P-values are denoted as follows: * p ≤ 0.05, ** p ≤ 0.01, and *** p ≤ 0.001.

The morphology of QCDs was studied with TEM. The samples were prepared by drop-casting of 0.1 mg/mL ethanol solution on a carbon support grid. Rounded-shaped nanodots, which were well-distributed on the carbon support grid were identified ( Fig. 1A). An average diameter of 2.6 ± 0.4 nm was estimated by examining the size of 70 identified QCDs (Fig. 1B). The XRD spectra of both quercetin and QCDs were measured in the film-state by drop-casting from respective 0.1 mg/mL ethanol solutions ( Fig. 2A). Quercetin possesses a highly crystalline structure as indicated by sharp diffraction peaks observed at 10.7°, 12.3°, 15.8°, 24.4°, 26.60°, and 27.50° [31,32]. In contrast, the crystallinity of QCDs was lower as manifested by the fewer recognizable peaks and a broad peak emerging in the range of 18°–30°. We speculate that the decrease in crystallinity compared with the precursor quercetin is due to the reduced planarity of QCDs, resulting from their nanodot-like structure [33,34].

FTIR spectra of quercetin and QCDs in powdered state and their corresponding vibrational band assignments are shown in Fig. 2B. Quercetin identified prominent vibrational stretching peak at 3286 cm−1 which is ascribed to -OH groups in quercetin [32]. Bands at 1660 and 1607 cm−1 are identified to CO groups which originated from the aryl ketonic group in quercetin [32]. Additional bands at 1560 and 1506 cm−1 are attributed to CC bonds which presented in the aromatic rings of quercetin [32]. C-H bands identified at 1360 and 1317 cm−1 are attributed to the in plane aromatic hydrocarbon vibrations in quercetin structure [32]

UV–vis absorption spectra of both quercetin and QCDs measured in 0.1 mg/mL ethanol solutions are shown in Fig. 3a. The spectrum of quercetin revealed two prominent peaks. The first peak was at 256 nm which is attributed to the π-π* transition of the benzoyl moiety in its structure [35,36]. A second absorption peak occurred at 372 nm which corresponded to the n-π* transitions in cinnamoyl portion of its structure [35,36] However, three absorption peaks were found in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of QCDs An absorption peaks at 256 nm and 292 nm are related to the π-π* transitions arising from the benzoyl moiety in quercetin and the aromatic structure located on the carbon core of QCDs respectively [35–37] A third absorption peak occurred at 365 nm, had relatively lower intensity and was slightly blue-shifted when compared to that of quercetin. This peak is also attributed to the n-π* transitions from the cinnamoyl groups [36,37] We note there is a slight blue shift and lower absorption intensity, which could infer a reduction in conjugation length during the formation of QCDs. Another possible reason for this blue shift is the loss of hydrophilic groups resulting from the de-hydroxylation reaction [37].

Quercetin is known to exhibit weak fluorescence in solution due to dual florescent behaviour which results from the intramolecular excited state proton transfer phenomenon [38]. The PL spectra of quercetin and QCDs excited by wavelength of 340 nm are shown in Fig. 3b. The emission profiles for quercetin and QCDs were markedly different with quercetin demonstrating comparatively broader emission profile with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 185 nm and a peak emission at 536 nm. QCDs on the other hand, showed a relatively narrower emission profile with FWHM of 94 nm and a peak emission at 447 nm. Previous studies have examined the molecular rotation of quercetin. The distinct optical properties observed in our study suggest that the intrinsic molecular rotation of quercetin in solution is likely inhibited by intramolecular restrictions within the QCDs [31].

The antioxidant activities of quercetin and QCDs were evaluated using 2,2´-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) free radicals scavenging assays. The antioxidant potential of QCDs is correlated with the particle’s concentrations ( Fig. 4A, B). Quercetin and QCDs demonstrated approximately 65 % and 92 % scavenging activity in the ABTS assay, respectively. Similarly, in the DPPH assay, Quercetin displayed 51 % scavenging activity and QCDs exhibited approximately 72 % scavenging activity when studied at concentration of 3 mg/mL.

These findings are consistent with previous studies that have highlighted the antioxidant properties of quercetin and its derivatives [39–42]. Previous studies have reported the role of antioxidants in enhancing cellular functions, including cell proliferation and migration [43–45]. Therefore, we hypothesised that the QCDs could affect proliferation, migration, and differentiation of cultured Schwann cells and support neurite outgrowth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of QCDs on Schwann cells viability and proliferation
ce:section.ce:para: We evaluated the impact of quercetin and QCDs on Schwann cell viability and proliferation using Live/Dead and AlamarBlue assays ( Fig. 5). The Live/Dead assay utilized calcein AM staining for live cells (green fluorescence) and propidium iodide (PI, red fluorescence) staining for dead cells following treatment with different concentrations of quercetin and QCDs. In control cultures, very few PI-positive dead cells were found (1.4 % ± 0.3) and neither Quercetin (Fig. 5A-D) nor QCDs (Fig. 5E-H) negatively affected cell survival.

These findings are in line with previous studies that have emphasized the low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility of CDs. The stability of photoluminescence in CDs under various conditions [39], the incorporation of porphyrins to enhance biocompatibility [39], and the use of CDs for cellular imaging with minimal cytotoxicity [40] further support the findings of this study.
The AlamarBlue assay was used to evaluate Schwann cell proliferation following treatment with quercetin and QCDs at different concentrations after 1, 3, and 5 days in culture (Fig. 5I).

These observations suggest that QCDs are significantly more efficient in supporting cell proliferation than quercetin (Fig. 5I) which agrees with previous studies that carbon dots are biocompatible, promote cell proliferation, and potentially could be used in various biomedical applications [46–49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: QCDs effects on Schwann cell migration
ce:section.ce:para: The wound healing assay showed that Schwann cell migration was significantly enhanced by QCDs treatment at all tested concentrations ( Fig. 6A–H and M). However, there was no statistical difference between the 0.3 and 0.75 mg/mL concentrations of QCDs. These findings were further supported by the results from the transwell migration assay (Fig. 6I–L and N). These observations suggested that QCDs have the potential to promote both migration and invasion of Schwann cells, which are important for nerve regeneration [50].

The present observations are consistent with previous research that underscores the potential of carbon dots in promoting cellular processes, particularly in promoting cell migration and neurite outgrowth [51,52]. Carbon dots are known for their antioxidant properties which could positively impact cell migration [51,52].
It is well established that different factors including neurotrophins could influence Schwann cell migration [53].
Therefore, in our next experiments, we examined changes in gene expression of different growth factors and neurite outgrowth from NG108-15 cells using QCDs at 0.3 mg/mL and 0.75 mg/mL concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of QCDs on the expression of growth factors in cultured Schwann cells
ce:section.ce:para: We compared the trophic factor gene expression profiles of untreated control Schwann cells cultures with those of the cultures treated with QCDS at 0.3 mg/mL and 0.75 mg/mL concentrations, respectively ( Fig. 7). We found statistically significant increases in the expression of neurotrophin 3 (NT-3), brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), glial cell line-derived neurotrophic factor (GDNF) and vascular endothelial growth factor-A (VEGFA) when QCDs were applied at 0,3 and 0.75 mg/mL concentration. Expression of nerve growth factor (NGF) was increased only following treatment with QCDs at 0.75 mg/mL concentration.

This upregulation of neurotrophic and angiogenic growth factors suggests a potential role of QCDs in promoting the proliferation, migration, and differentiation of Schwann cells. These findings are supported by previous reports on the importance of neurotrophic factors for the regulation of Schwann cell behaviour and peripheral nerve regeneration [54–56]. Schwann cells proliferate, migrate along axons, and form the myelin sheath, underscoring the importance of factors regulating these processes [57,58].

NT3 plays a crucial role in influencing Schwann cell migration [59,60]. These neurotrophins activate signalling pathways like the Rho family GTPases, Rac1, and Cdc42, which are essential for promoting cell migration [61].

Moreover, the upregulation of VEGFA indicates that QCDs may also influence the vascularization of regenerating peripheral nerves, which is essential for regeneration and function recovery [62–64]. The promotion of angiogenesis by QCDs could enhance the delivery of oxygen and nutrients to regenerating nerves, facilitating the overall repair process [65–67].

Co-culture of NG108-15 cells with QCDs for 24 h at a concentration of 0.75 mg/mL revealed that some carbon particles were associated with the cell membranes and could be visualized using fluorescent microscopy ( Fig. 8A-C).

Administration of QCDs at 0.3 mg/mL and 0.75 mg/mL concentrations supported enhanced neurite outgrowth from the NG108-15 cells (Fig. 8D-H). The longest neurites were 183 ± 9 μm and 245 ± 6 μm with 0.3 mg/mL and 0.75 mg/mL, respectively. The neurite length in control cultures was 106 ± 7 μm. In addition, QCDs at concentration of 0.75 mg/mL significantly increased the percentage of cells with neurites (Fig. 8G). The results indicate a clear dose-dependent enhancement, with the 0.75 mg/mL concentration showing a more pronounced effect significantly compared to the 0.3 mg/mL concentration.

These results are consistent with previous research indicating that carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene, promote neurite outgrowth in various neuronal cell types [68]. Studies have also highlighted the potential of functionalized CNTs to stimulate neurite outgrowth [69]. Additionally, the use of carbon nanotube-hydrogel composites combined with electrical stimulation has been shown to enhance neurite outgrowth [70]. Additionally, it has been shown that carbon dots could modulate different intracellular signalling pathways like the Ras-ERK pathway, which is known to support neurite outgrowth [59,61].

The observed increase in neurite outgrowth with QCDs treatment could be attributed to the ability of carbon nanomaterials to provide substrates that support neuronal growth and differentiation [71]. Moreover, previous reports suggest that compounds like quercetin can stimulate neurite outgrowth in neuronal cells [72]. Quercetin has also been shown to exert neuroprotective effects and promote neurotrophic factor expression, which may contribute to enhanced neurite outgrowth [73]. Additionally, a recent study has demonstrated effects of quercetin on neurite outgrowth [74].

The synthesis and characterization of QCDs in our study represents a significant advancement in the field of nanomedicine, particularly in the context of nerve regeneration therapies. The multifaceted properties of QCDs, including their antioxidant capabilities and their ability to stimulate Schwann cell activity with expression of neurotrophic factors, offer promising potential for treating peripheral nerve injuries. The dose-dependent free radical scavenging effect demonstrated by QCDs in DPPH and ABTS assays is particularly noteworthy. Oxidative stress plays a crucial role in the pathophysiology of nerve injuries, and the ability of QCDs to neutralize free radicals could provide significant neuroprotection [75,76]. This antioxidant effect may help mitigate secondary neuronal damage following nerve injury, creating a more favorable environment for regeneration.

The observed promotion of Schwann cell proliferation and migration by QCDs is a key finding with direct implications for nerve regeneration. Schwann cells are essential for successful peripheral nerve repair, as they provide trophic support, guide axonal regrowth, and remyelinate regenerating axons [77]. By enhancing Schwann cell activity, QCDs could potentially accelerate and improve the quality of nerve regeneration.

The upregulation of neurotrophic and angiogenic growth factors induced by QCDs is another crucial aspect of their potential therapeutic value. Neurotrophic factors such as NGF and BDNF are vital for axonal growth and neuronal survival [78]. Similarly, angiogenic factors promote the formation of new blood vessels, which is essential for supplying nutrients and oxygen to regenerating nerves [79]. The ability of QCDs to stimulate the expression of these factors could create a more supportive microenvironment for nerve regeneration.

The stimulation of neurite outgrowth from NG108-15 cells by QCDs further underscores their potential in promoting nerve repair. This effect suggests that QCDs may directly influence neuronal cells, potentially enhancing axonal regrowth following injury [79]. The combination of Schwann cell activation and direct neuronal effects positions QCDs as a multifaceted therapeutic agent for peripheral nerve repair.

The findings of this study open several avenues for potential clinical applications. QCDs could be incorporated into nerve guidance conduits or hydrogels to create bioactive scaffolds for nerve repair [80]. Additionally, they might be used as a standalone therapy or in combination with existing treatments to enhance outcomes in peripheral nerve injuries.

While these results are promising, further research is needed to fully elucidate the mechanisms by which QCDs exert their effects on Schwann cells and neurons. In vivo studies will be crucial to validate the efficacy and safety of QCDs in animal models of peripheral nerve injury. Additionally, investigating the long-term effects of QCD treatment and optimizing their delivery methods will be important steps towards clinical translation.

This study presented a facile solvothermal synthesis of QCDs from quercetin hydrate precursor. The resulting QCDs exhibited a bright luminescence in solution and a remarkable dispersibility in water owing to their manometer-scale size and abundance of hydrophilic groups on the surface of QCDs, enhancing their bioavailability. These merits enable the investigation of QCDs potential application in biomedical applications.

The results demonstrated that QCDs have positive effects on Schwann cells, promoting their proliferation and migration as well as expression of neurotrophic and angiogenic factors. In addition, QCDs enhance neurite outgrowth.

By harnessing the antioxidant properties and cellular interaction capabilities of carbon dots, it could be possible to explore innovative approaches to promote nerve regeneration and functional recovery. Further research into the molecular mechanisms underlying the effects of carbon dots is necessary to advance our comprehension and explore potential therapeutic applications in nerve regeneration.
In summary, this study underscores the potential of QCDs as a novel and promising platform for drug delivery, with implications for treating peripheral nerve injuries.
The animal study protocols were approved by the Animal Review Board at the Court of Appeal of Northern Norrland in Umeå, Sweden (ethical permits DNR #A15-20).
